coredata.dc:title: Efficient preparation of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor system based on aptamer-composite and detection of bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate in pork
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yueying
Li, Wenting
Hu, Xuetao
Zhang, Xinai
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Li, Miaoyun
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor system is proposed for detecting bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) in pork, which is based on aptamer recognition with molybdenum disulfide quantum dots and cadmium telluride quantum dots (MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta). Two signals exist in the system, among which the response signal is transmitted by CdTe-Apta. The amide condensation between aptamers and CdTe QDs shortens the distance between CdTe QDs and DEHP, thus quenching the fluorescence of CdTe QDs, possibly through a photoinduced electron transfer mechanism. The MoS2 QDs deliver the self-calibration signal, and the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs remains almost constant when co-existing with DEHP. Linearity (R2 = 0.9536) was established for the DEHP concentration range 0.005–3.0 mg·L−1, with a limit of detection of 0.21 μg·L−1. The system was successfully applied in the determination of DEHP in pork. The system has potential for the quantitative determination of DEHP in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide quantum dots
Cadmium telluride quantum dots
Aptamer
Bis (2-ethylhexyl) phthalate
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DEHP in pork samples
Feasibility and possible mechanism for DEHP assay
Detection of DEHP in pork samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The HRTEM images and (B) particle size distribution of MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta. (C) XRD pattern, and (D) FT-IR spectra of materials.

(A) The CD spectra of Apta with and without DEHP. (B) The emission spectra of MoS2 QDs, CdTe-Apta, and MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta without and with DEHP under 353 nm excitation wavelength. (C) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of DEHP with emission spectra of MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta. (D) The fluorescence lifetime of MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta in the presence or absence of DEHP. (E) The EHOMO and ELUMO of fluorophore and receptor respectively.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta at different DEHP concentrations. (B) The linear relationship between the response value (I575/I423) of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta and the DEHP concentration (0.005–3.0 mg L−1) (N = 3). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CdTe-Apta at different DEHP concentrations. (D) The linear relationship between the response value (I/I0) of CdTe-Apta and the DEHP concentration (0.2–2.2 mg L−1) (N = 3). (E) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram.

(A) Selectivity of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta in the presence of various interfering substances. (B) Deviation rate of the detection results of the CdTe-Apta and the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta at different temperatures. (C) The storage stability results of material at periodical intervals of 10 days over two months. (D) The Zeta potential of CdTe QDs, CdTe-Apta, and MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta.
Schematic illustration of MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta for the DEHP detection assay.
The recovery rate of DEHP in the pork samples using the fluorescence method and GC–MS.

head.ce:title: Efficient preparation of dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor system based on aptamer-composite and detection of bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate in pork

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Meat Processing and Safety International United Lab, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou 450000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
Henan Meat Processing and Safety International United Lab

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Henan Meat Processing and Safety International United Lab, Henan Agricultural University, Zhengzhou 450000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor system is proposed for detecting bis(2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) in pork, which is based on aptamer recognition with molybdenum disulfide quantum dots and cadmium telluride quantum dots (MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta). Two signals exist in the system, among which the response signal is transmitted by CdTe-Apta. The amide condensation between aptamers and CdTe QDs shortens the distance between CdTe QDs and DEHP, thus quenching the fluorescence of CdTe QDs, possibly through a photoinduced electron transfer mechanism. The MoS2 QDs deliver the self-calibration signal, and the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs remains almost constant when co-existing with DEHP. Linearity (R2 = 0.9536) was established for the DEHP concentration range 0.005–3.0 mg·L−1, with a limit of detection of 0.21 μg·L−1. The system was successfully applied in the determination of DEHP in pork. The system has potential for the quantitative determination of DEHP in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic products have brought convenience to human life, but the accompanying abuse phenomenon has intensified. Food safety problems caused by plastic packaging materials have occurred frequently, which has attracted the attention of the public (Crowley, 2020). Plasticizers, particularly phthalates, are widely used in plastics because they can ameliorate the processability of packaging materials (Xu, Kolapkar, Zinchik, Bar-Ziv, & McDonald, 2020). Phthalates added to plastics dissolve, migrate, and volatilize during processing and use, and they damage human health as well as the environment when released into the ecosystems (Choi, Lee, Lee, & Moon, 2020). The adverse effects of phthalates have been reported repeatedly. Many studies have verified that plasticizers can enter the human body and damage health through inhalation, skin absorption, and environmental pollution. A widely used phthalate plasticizer, the annual output of bis (2-ethylhexyl) phthalate (DEHP) has exceeded 2 million tons (Wu et al., 2020). When ingested through food contamination, DEHP will be converted into 2-ethylhexyl-phthalate in the intestines and then preferentially absorbed. Therefore, the European Union and the United States impose restrictions on the migration of DEHP from food contact materials (Ventrice, Ventrice, Russo, & De Sarro, 2013).

Various methods have been developed for determining phthalates. Most methods used for food are based on high performance /ultra–performance liquid chromatography (Lirio, Fu, Lin, Hsu, & Huang, 2016) or gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Cariou et al., 2016). However, column loss occurs when an extraction procedure for lipophilic compounds is used to analyze phthalates in complex sample matrices due to the high lipophilicity of phthalates (Chao, Liao, & Kuo, 2016). The detection signal may be affected by coelution isomer or matrix interference. Mass spectrometry has the advantages of high selectivity and sensitivity, but the instrument is complex and time-consuming. It requires laboratory conditions and specialist operation, which makes rapid and daily on-site detection difficult. Furthermore, these methods may reach specificity and sensitivity limits when used at trace levels far below specific migration limits (Cariou et al., 2016). Therefore, exploring a simple detection method with high efficiency is necessary.

Fluorescence analysis has attracted widespread attention in recent years due to its simplicity, rapidity, and good selectivity. However, the single-emission fluorescence method has certain limitations in stability. Studies have shown that the fluorescence detection signal is vulnerable to various analyte-irrelevant factors such as the background interference and detection environment (Yang, Li, Wang, Peng, Xiong, & Chen, 2019). The ratiometric fluorescence method uses the ratio of the intensity of two emission peaks as the detection signal. This method has a built-in environmental interference correction function that can greatly reduce the impact of analyte-irrelevant factors (Ju, Li, Liu, Han, Xiao, Luo, et al., 2018). Several ratiometric methods have recently been designed on the basis of quantum dots (Hao, Wei, Nie, Xu, Lu, Yan, et al., 2016; Zhang, Qian, Mei, Yang, He, Liu, et al., 2019). As a typical representative of transition metal sulfide, molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) have direct bandgap characteristics, which means they have excellent physical and photoelectric properties (Li, Shi, Hu, Li, Huang, Holmes, et al., 2019). Cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs) are among the most widely used II-VI semiconductor quantum dots. Ligand-stabilized CdTe has a high quantum yield and excellent photostability (Choudhary & Nageswaran, 2019). In addition, MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs have similar excitation wavelengths. No interference occurs between the two emission spectra, and the combination of peaks is perfect. A dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor system constructed by combining MoS2 QDs with CdTe QDs consequently has great potential.

In the current study, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor system based on aptamer recognition was established (MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta), which was used to detect DEHP in pork firstly. The CdTe-Apta and the MoS2 QDs act as the response signal and the self-calibration signal, respectively. On the basis of the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two signals, the quantitative analysis of DEHP was conducted. Several experimental parameters were optimized, and the specificity and stability of the system were investigated systematically. Furthermore, the possible detection mechanism is explored in detail. Finally, the proposed method is further verified by GC–MS, with satisfactory results. Therefore, the method proposed in this study has practical significance for the detection of DEHP contamination in pork.

Glutathione (GSH) and sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Surface carboxylated CdTe QDs were obtained from Xi’an qiyue Biological Technology (Xi’an, China). DEHP aptamer (Apta) (5′- NH2-C6-AAC GGA AGC GCG AAG CTT GCG C-3′) was supplied by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). Methanol, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), acetonitrile, n-hexane, and nine types of phthalate esters (detailed information in Table S2) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Fresh pork was purchased from the metro supermarket (Zhenjiang, China). All chemical reagents were analytical pure grade, and the experimental water employed was ultrapure.

All fluorescent spectra were obtained using an F-98 fluorescence spectrometer (Lengguang Technology, Shanghai, China). Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were captured using a UV-1601 spectrometer (Beifen-Ruili, Beijing, China). Materials morphology and structure were characterized through a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). Particle distribution and zeta potential were estimated using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Panalytical, Malvern, UK). A J-815 circular dichroism (CD) spectrometer (Jasco, Tokyo, Japan) was introduced to characterize the interaction between DEHP and aptamers. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were evaluated with a D8 advance diffractometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo, MA, USA). Fluorescence lifetime was measured using QuantaMaster 40 fluorescence lifetime spectrometers (PTI, USA). GC–MS (Agilent, USA) was used as a certified method for all samples.

MoS2 QDs were synthesized by employing the hydrothermal method, based on existing literature (Li et al., 2019). First, 0.25 g of Na2MoO4·2H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 10 min under ultrasonication. The solution pH was then adjusted to 7 with 0.1 M HCl. Subsequently, 0.5 g of GSH was added to the solution, and ultrasonic stirring was performed for 10 min, after which the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 220 °C at a rate of 15 °C/min, and maintained for 24 h. Then, the solution was naturally cooled to room temperature in a fume hood, centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant, and filtered three times with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove larger particles. Finally, purified MoS2 QDs were preserved at 4 °C for further use.

The CdTe-Apta was prepared through a coupling reaction using the EDC/NHS technique. Specifically, 500 μL of EDC/NHS aqueous solution (10 mg/mL for each) was added to 2 mL of CdTe QDs solution, followed by stirring for 30 min at 4 °C to activate the carboxyl group. Then, Apta solution (15 μL with 100 μM) was added, and the mixture was incubated under vigorous stirring for 1 h. CdTe-Apta was thus successfully prepared.

For DEHP detection, 2 mL of CdTe-Apta were mixed with 2 mL of MoS2 QDs under ultrasonication for 5 min, and the mixture was then incubated under magnetic stirring for 10 min, which was denoted as a MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta ratiometric fluorescence sensing system. DEHP with various concentrations was added to the system. After that, the mixture was diluted with PBS to a final volume of 1000 μL and incubated at 25 ℃ for 10 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded by the fluorescence spectrometer (λex = 353 nm), and all measurements were performed three times. Fluorescence intensity (I575/I423) was calculated for quantitative analysis of DEHP concentration. A schematic of the detection mechanism is provided in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DEHP in pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: DEHP in pork samples was detected. First, 1.0 g of fresh pork was cut into small pieces. It was added to a mixture containing 5 mL of methanol (70%) and 5 mM EDTA, and then homogenized for 10 min and immersed in an ultrasonic water bath for 1 h. The supernatant was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. Acetonitrile was added (acetonitrile: supernatant = 3:2), and the mixture was stirred violently for 30 min and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove the protein. Finally, the supernatant was evaporated in a water bath at 80 °C to remove the acetonitrile (Wang, Mei, Tao, Wu, Zhao, Wang, et al., 2020). DEHP of various concentrations was added to the pork sample for further analysis. GC–MS was used (as the standard method) for the detection of phthalate esters, and the detection performance of the proposed fluorescence method was verified (the instrumental parameters for GC–MS measurements as shown in Table S3). All pork samples were tested three times, and the results for each sample were averaged.

The fluorescence quantum yield of MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs were calculated to be 20.73% and 27.77%, respectively (The specific procedure is provided in the supplementary material). Fig. S2(A) depicts the fluorescence emission spectra of MoS2 QDs, CdTe QDs, and MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta under 353 nm excitation wavelength. The peaks at 423 and 575 nm correspond to the fluorescence emissions of MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs, respectively. The MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta ratiometric fluorescence sensor system also exhibited dual-emission peaks at 423 and 575 nm.

Subsequently, to obtain the optimal excitation wavelength for the sensing system, emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths were determined through three-dimensional scanning with the fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. S2(B), when the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 350 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the dual-emission peaks was magnified gradually. At the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the MoS2 QDs emission peak reached its maximum. When the excitation wavelength was increased further, the fluorescence intensity of the MoS2 QDs emission peak began to decrease. The fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs emission peak continued to increase and reached its peak at the 360 nm excitation wavelength. In consideration of the excitation wavelength–dependent fluorescence emission behavior of the dual-emission peak, 1 nm was selected as the step size of the excitation wavelength for further study. As Fig. S2(C) depicts, the emission peak of the entire sensor system was optimal at the excitation wavelength of 353 nm. In addition, with increases of the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence peak position of MoS2 QDs shifted red clearly. This excitation wavelength–dependent behavior can be explained by the polydispersity of the QDs, and different particle sizes or diverse surface emissive trap sites (Fan, Zhang, Li, Liu, Liu, Li, et al., 2017; Mishra, Singh, Singh, Singh, Srikrishna, & Srivastava, 2018).

Material morphology and dimensions were characterized using HRTEM. MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs are nearly spherical and are uniformly dispersed, as shown in Fig. S3(A) and (B), respectively. The particle size distributions of the MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs were measured by the Zetasizer Nano. As in the illustrations of Fig. S3(A) and (B), the particle size distribution range of MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs are 3–9 and 1–6 nm, which is consistent with HRTEM results. Furthermore, experiments were performed to determine that aptamers were immobilized on CdTe QDs (details in the supplementary material). As shown in Fig. 1 (A) and (B), MoS2 QDs and CdTe-Apta are evenly mixed with no agglomeration after ultrasonic and magnetic stirring. The 0.21 nm (above) and 0.29 nm (below) lattices shown in the illustration correspond to Mo (110) and CdTe (100), respectively, which is consistent with previous reports (Duan, Zhang, Yang, Zhang, He, & Wang, 2018; Li et al., 2019; Nicholson, Martinez, Shah, Thiyagarajan, & Sampath, 2020). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta (Fig. S3(C–F)) further verified that Mo, S, Cd, and Te elements are uniformly assembled. These results indicate that the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta sensing system was in a stable state.

The crystal structures of the materials were further verified by XRD spectra (Alderton, 2021). As shown in Fig. 1(C), the positions and relative strengths of the diffraction peaks of 18.6° (100), 20.8° (002), 32.1° (102), and 38.9° (110) were well correlated with the cubic phase of CdTe QDs (Chen, Liu, Liu, Liu, Bian, Tian, et al., 2020). The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.1°, 33.2°, 40.2°, and 58.9° were accurately indexed to the (002), (100), (103) and (110) planes of MoS2 hexagonal phase, respectively (Li et al., 2019; Wang, 2018; Ze, Yueqiu, Xujun, & Yong, 2017). The XRD spectra demonstrated the successful synthesis of MoS2 QDs and CdTe QDs, which further confirmed HRTEM results.

For insight into the functional group composition of the materials, FT-IR spectra were performed. In Fig. 1(D), the spectra of CdTe-Apta exhibits characteristic peaks at 3447 and 1399 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the OH band and CO, respectively (Chen, et al., 2020). Moreover, the peak at 1126 cm−1 is attributable to the asymmetric CN stretching vibration of the amine group. The peak at 1588 cm−1 was assigned to the coupling reaction between NH and CN carbonyl, which was not observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CdTe QDs (Fig. S4(C)), thus further verifying the successful coupling of CdTe QDs with aptamers (Palanisamy, Nguyen, Nguyen, Nguyen, Bhuvaneswari, Sivashanmugan, et al., 2020; Yang, Zhao, Lu, Xu, Xu, Dong, et al., 2011). According to the spectra of MoS2 QDs, except for peaks that were similar to CdTe-Apta, other characteristic peaks such as that at 1668 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CO, and the peaks of 1408 and 1114 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CN. In addition, the peak at 1297 cm−1 is attributable to CO stretching vibration (Li, Zhang, Sun, Zhang, & Su, 2021; Zhang, Wang, Zhang, & Su, 2019). These results confirm that the surfaces of materials were modified with active groups, thus improving the stability of the system (Li et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and possible mechanism for DEHP assay
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between DEHP and aptamers was explored. As can be observed in Fig. 2 (A), after the addition of DEHP, the positive peaks at 230 and 280 nm were decreased compared with Apta alone, whereas the negative peak at 250 nm signified an opposite trend. This result indicates that Apta successfully binds to DEHP and changes its conformation, which inhibits the strong π-π* transition between base and deoxyribose, resulting in a decrease of CD peak (Lim, Kim, Yoon, You, Chua, & Son, 2018; Wen, Wang, Yuan, Liang, & Qiu, 2018). Then, the feasibility of the proposed method was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 2(B), under the excitation wavelength of 353 nm, when DEHP was added to MoS2 QDs, the fluorescence emission peak of MoS2 QDs at 423 nm remained almost unchanged, indicating that DEHP has no or a negligible effect on the fluorescence of MoS2 QDs. By contrast, adding the same amount of DEHP caused the fluorescence intensity of CdTe-Apta to decrease sharply. For the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta, when DEHP was added to the system, the fluorescence intensity at the peak position at 575 nm decreased significantly, whereas the change in fluorescence intensity at 423 nm was negligible. These results demonstrate that the established method is feasible for DEHP detection and can provide an internal correction to reduce error caused by analyte-irrelevant factors.

To elucidate the possible mechanism of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta quenching induced by DEHP, a series of experiments was conducted. First, as shown in Fig. 2(C), the UV–vis absorption peak of DEHP is clearly at 270 nm, whereas the fluorescence emission spectra of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta are at 423 and 575 nm. There is obviously no overlap between the spectra, which rules out Förster resonance energy transfer and the inner filter effect as possible mechanisms for the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta fluorescence quenching (Stanisavljevic, Krizkova, Vaculovicova, Kizek, & Adam, 2015). Through measurement of the excited-state lifetime of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta in DEHP presence and absence, the fluorescence dynamics can be effectively elucidated. The two decay curves were well fitted with di-exponential decay functions. The decay curve of the system (Fig. 2(D)) displays a fluorescence lifetime of 20.12 μs, which was decreased to 17.29 μs after DEHP was added. The fluorescence lifetimes without and with DEHP markedly differ, and this result confirms that the entire process is due to energy transfer (Huang, He, He, Zhang, Peng, Lin, et al., 2019). Therefore, we speculate that the possible mechanism of DEHP in quenching the fluorescence of the system involves photoinduced electron transfer (PET).

PET includes reductive PET and oxidative PET. In reductive PET, fluorophore acts as an electron acceptor to obtain electrons from an electron donor. Oxidative PET does the opposite (Zu, Yan, Bai, Xu, Wang, Huang, et al., 2017). To obtain further insight into the possible quenching mechanism, the Gaussian 09 program suite was used for theoretical calculation (Yuan, Xu, Zhao, Zhang, Lu, Shen, et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 2(E), DEHP possesses EHOMO (−6.14 eV), which is between EHOMO (−6.85 eV) and ELUMO (−2.38 eV) of the CdTe QDs fluorophores. This indicates that when the fluorophore is excited, the electron transfers from the quencher to the fluorophore, thereby quenching the fluorescence of CdTe QDs by reductive PET. The charge density and molecular orbital energy level of MoS2 QDs are different from those of CdTe QDs. The EHOMO of MoS2 QDs is −3.43 eV, which is greater than the EHOMO of DEHP. When MoS2 QDs are excited, DEHP does not affect its fluorophores, and the change in the fluorescence signal of MoS2 QDs can be ignored. In addition, the distance between the quencher and the fluorophore plays a dominant role in electron transfer efficiency, which decreases proportionally to increasing distance (Huang, et al., 2019; Lu, Wang, Zhao, Sun, & Yang, 2017; Zhang, Zhu, Guo, Li, Li, & Wang, 2013).

The fluorescence properties are affected not only by their molecular structure, but also by their concentration and environmental factors (He, Pan, Cao, Yue, Wang, & Liang, 2018). Initially, for finding the optimal Apta addition, the influence of Apta concentration on the response signal was explored in detail, and the fluorescence intensity ratio at 423 nm in the presence or absence of DEHP was taken as the response signal. As shown in Fig. S5(A), the response signal increases initially and then reaches a maximum with an increase in Apta concentration from 0 to 1.5 μM, followed by an attenuation trend. This may be because the high surface density aptamers not only hinder the binding of DEHP with aptamers but also restrict interfacial electron transfer (Zhang, Zhu, Jiang, Wang, Guo, Shi, et al., 2020). Therefore, 1.5 μM was selected as the optimal concentration of Apta.

To determine the optimum detection conditions of DEHP, the effects of pH (4–11) and incubation time (0–25 min) on the detection results were investigated in detail with I575/I423 as the response signal. As depicted by Fig. S5(B), the response signal changes with continuous adjustment of the pH of the detection solution. When the pH increases from acidic to weakly alkaline, the response signal increases gradually. At pH 8, the response signal reaches the peak, but the signal decreases as the pH continues to strong alkalinity. This phenomenon suggests that strong acidity or strong alkalinity can change the system potential, thus affecting the stability of the fluorescence system (Li, Hu, Li, Shi, Zhai, Xu, et al., 2020). Accordingly, pH 8 was chosen for the subsequent experiments. Fig. S5(C) displays the influence of DEHP incubation time in the sensing system on the response signal. The response signal tended to be stable when DEHP was incubated for 10 min. When the incubation time is prolonged, the change of the response signal is negligible. In subsequent experiments, 10 min was selected as the optimal incubation time for stable and accurate experimental results.

After the optimization of the experimental parameters, the concentration of DEHP was detected quantitatively using the proposed method. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 (A), with the increase of DEHP concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs at 575 nm decreases consistently, but that of MoS2 QDs at 423 nm is hardly affected. Fig. 3(B) illustrates the good linear correlation between I575/I423 and different DEHP concentrations in the range of 0.005–3.0 mg·L−1. The corresponding linear regression equation is
y = - 0.17494 x + 0.80993
R
2
= 0.9536
, where y represents the I575/I423 value and × represents the DEHP concentration. Based on the three times the standard deviation principle, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.21 μg·L−1. The detection limit of this method is far lower than the detection threshold of DEHP in pork, which is 1.5 mg kg−1, according to China national standard. Moreover, the sensitivity of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta fluorescence sensor system to DEHP was compared with that for the CdTe-Apta (Fig. 3(C) and (D)). As the results reveal, dual-emission fluorescence has a higher detection sensitivity and wider linear range than single-emission fluorescence.

The fluorescence changes from red to blue in the solution can be clearly observed under UV light and were further verified by the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (Fig. 3(E)). MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta emits a rose-red fluorescence with CIE coordinates of (0.3004, 0.2853). With enlargement of the amount of DEHP additive, the CIE coordinates shift to blue (0.2475, 0.2040), which is expected to enable visual detection. In addition, as shown in Table S4, the method proposed in the present study has a lower LOD and a wider linear detection range than those observed in most previous studies. Therefore, the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta ratiometric fluorescence detection method is ideal for the quantitative determination of DEHP.

Selectivity is a vital indicator of the detection performance of a method. To investigate the selectivity of the proposed method, eight other types of phthalate esters (DMP, DEP, DIBP, DBP, DPP, DHXP, BBP, and DNP) were selected as interfering substances. Analogs with linear alkyl groups, isoalkyl groups, and aryl groups are represented by dark gray, light gray, and dark blue columns, respectively, in Fig. 4 (A). In the presence of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta, most of the interfering substances had no or an imperceptible quenching effect, whereas a strong quenching effect was observed after the addition of DEHP. When interfering substances were introduced into solution containing DEHP, the presence of the interfering substances had no clear influence on the fluorescence response signal (I575/I423), which suggests adequate selectivity and specificity for DEHP. A possible reason is that the aptamers coupled to the surface of CdTe QDs through amide condensation specifically recognize DEHP, whereas other plasticizers cannot interact with CdTe QDs through specific recognition, thus expanding the distance between them and inhibiting PET (Zhang et al., 2013). These results are consistent with “electron tunneling theory” and “self-consistent field theory” and further attest to the distance-dependent mechanism of PET (Huang, et al., 2019; Lu, Wang, Zhao, Sun, & Yang, 2017).

The stability of the fluorescent material is another crucial indicator of its performance. In the current study, its stability was studied from the aspects of temperature and storage duration. First, the stability of the dual-emission MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta ratiometric fluorescence sensor system and single-emission CdTe QDs with changes in ambient temperature was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of the tested solution were measured and recorded when the temperature of the system was increased from 0 to 30 ℃ at periodical intervals of 5 ℃. The stability of the fluorescent materials was measured by the deviation rate of the results. The deviation rate formula (1) is as follows (Li, et al., 2020):
(1)
D r
=
C -
C 0
C 0
× 100 %
where Dr is the deviation rate, C refers to the DEHP concentration measured by the fluorescence method at different temperatures, and C0 is the reference DEHP concentration.

The effect of temperature change on the stability of the system is presented in Fig. 4(B). The fluorescence intensity decreased slightly with the increase in temperature, which could be explained by the increased rate of electronic energy converting into vibrational energy (Jana, Inamdar, & Suresh Kumar, 2017). The deviation rates of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta and the single-emission CdTe-Apta were 0.9%–4.6% and 2.7%–7.1%, respectively. The MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta exhibited higher detection stability than the single-emission CdTe-Apta did in different ambient temperatures. The dominant reason is that in the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta, the built-in calibration signal can self-adjust, thereby reducing instability in the detection results caused by temperature change (Li et al., 2020).

The storage stability of the material was tested at periodical intervals of 10 days over two months. As may be seen in Fig. 4(C), the normalized fluorescence intensity changed, which may be caused by the easy inactivation of the aptamer. However, the magnitude of change was not significant, which means the particles are soluble and stable in the water as before. The solubility and stability of the MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta are mainly due to surface passivation and improved hydrophilicity in the presence of –COOH, –NH2, SO2 –, and other functional groups, which were confirmed by FT-IR spectra. Because these functional groups are electron-rich, the conclusion can be further verified by detecting zeta potential. As Fig. 4(D) depicts, the measured zeta potential of CdTe QDs, CdTe-Apta, and MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta were –19.7, –26.4, and –31.1 mV, respectively, indicating that their surfaces are negatively charged. Due to the existence of the negative charge, electrostatic repulsion (surface passivation) among particles prevents aggregation, thus it exhibits high dispersion and stability in the aqueous medium (Mishra, Singh, Singh, Singh, Srikrishna, & Srivastava, 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DEHP in pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was applied in DEHP detection in pork samples to evaluate its practicability, and its performance was verified using GC–MS. DEHP was not detected in the processed pork samples. After complex pretreatment, different amounts of DEHP standard solution were spiked into pork samples for determination. The recoveries of the spiked samples by GC–MS and the proposed method were in the range of 87.0%–113.5% and 89.5%–122.0% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was in the range of 1.4%–5.2% and 2.6%–7.1% (Table 1 ). Although the RSD values are slightly higher than the traditional methods, the level of the RSD is still acceptable for trace detection (Liu, Zhang, He, Gao, & Shi, 2020). Therefore, quantitative detection of DEHP can be obtained by the method, implying promising applications in actual sample detection. However, some obstacles and challenges still exist for widespread applications, due to the complexity of preprocessing and the insufficient accuracy of detection.

We constructed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor system using CdTe QDs with DEHP specific aptamers attached to the surface and MoS2 QDs and we used it to detect DEHP quantities in pork. Under optimal conditions, linearity toward DEHP was obtained in the concentration range of 0.005–3.0 mg·L−1 along with a LOD of 0.21 μg·L−1. In addition, the self-calibration function of the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor system effectively eliminates interference in a complex matrix and improves the stability of the detection result. Finally, the proposed method was successfully used for DEHP detection in pork after complex pretreatment, which was consistent with the detection result of the GC–MS method. To summarizes, the proposed MoS2 QDs/CdTe-Apta has broad prospects in applications for DEHP detection in actual samples.

Yueying Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Wenting Li: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xuetao Hu: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Xinai Zhang: Formal analysis. Xiaowei Huang: Formal analysis. Zhihua Li: Validation, Investigation. Miaoyun Li: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision. Jiyong Shi: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by all the funds.

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (grant numbers 2018YFD0400800); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 31772073, 31671844); the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant numbers BE2019359); Distinguished Professor Foundation of Jiangsu Province (grant number 202074); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.129352.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
